speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Ruth Crane. This is tape four. Ruth, you were talking about your father going off to Bedzin in forced labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father happened to be-- on those two days-- back home-- back with us. This is where it was our home then.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long had he been in Bedzin for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Look, it was in-- how long has he been in Bedzin? A few months-- yes, you see--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --a few months. And he was-- he was-- 2-- he was 2 weeks, and-- and then a day and a 1/2 or 2 days in Olkusz. And the rest was in Bedzin. So he had a pass that he could travel, yes? And occasionally, we went with-- with him to the train. And I remember that always, the last train-- if you went with the last train, it was 5 to 4:00 when the train leaved-- or leaved the-- the station-- or whatever they call the station-- or 5 o'clock in the morning-- first thing it was in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, on this particular day, he was at home. He was with us. And suddenly, we heard the sound of boots running up those steps. We heard the sound of boots. And we knew what it meant. Those boots belonged to-- to the German guards.

speaker: interviewee
text: They ran up the steps, and they burst into the small kitchenette.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three. Now, it was a very tiny kitchenette. This whole flat was small. They pushed us. They didn't say a word. They pushed us all into a little passage. This was the kitchenette, and the bedrooms were off on the right-hand side. Something was on the left-hand side. He pushed us all into the left-hand side. They pushed us, and they started to search feverishly. They took the whole-- literally-- you know-- they searched everywhere that they could think of. We didn't know why they searched. Or maybe my father suspected-- as he was occasionally-- when he was coming in, he was bringing bits and pieces of jewelry and what were coins. So-- and there was somebody-- I was the go-between. There was just like a little street, and there was somebody waiting. And that was for to take it in exchange of bits of food and some information. And I knew one thing. I was the-- that was-- if my father told me that next time he's going to bring something in, it was outside. It was between him and me. And he-- he put a lot of emphasis on it-- that nobody else must know it. Because those people in that flat, they were starving. We were all starving. So we were prepared. They were, as adults-- they were prepared to do anything to-- to prolong the life. They didn't think about the quality of life, you know-- just to have something there. So this was apparently-- he felt this was-- or they felt-- I don't think so that they knew when there were bits and pieces of bread or bits and pieces of food coming. This was a time when a couple of bites, you know, made the difference between everything. So they didn't know. Just whatever-- whatever somebody brought in, whoever was in the flat-- in this flat, not the rest of it, because there was only always little bits and pieces-- we shared it. And so, as far as I knew, it was myself and my father. Nobody else knew. And so when they ran up, perhaps my-- my-- my father suspected something. I have no idea. But they didn't find anything. And then they-- next, they took Lilka into-- on the left, there, into that place, into that little-- whatever that was-- a little pantry or whatever-- like, a little room it was there. And they said to us-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --no sound. If we hear one sound, if we hear a cry, or a yell, or anything, she is going-- she is going to be no Lilka no more-- not a sound from you. And then next we heard-- the-- the sounds of her being beaten. And they kept saying, tell us. We know that your father-- or we know that things are come-- your father was very wealthy-- your parents were very wealthy. We know that there is extras here. We want to know where it's coming from. You-- you know. Tell us. And she kept whimpering. She kept saying, I don't know anything. I'm starving. I'm just a child. I don't remember even from home. We don't have anything. Please let me be. I don't know anything. I don't know. She kept repeating again and again while she was beaten. And we couldn't do a thing. After a long, long time-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --the door opened. I don't know how many hours it was. Maybe it wasn't hours. I don't know. But it was hours, but I don't know how many. [SIGHS] They threw her on the ground, and they left without a backward glance-- without a backward glance. For the next two weeks-- I mean-- that they didn't kill her, that she survived, I really don't know how. For the next two weeks, all I remember is watching her. And I looked at her. And I watched her. And I looked at her. And I could not believe-- and my father had to go back, of course. And I could not believe that she is there. And I petted her, you know. And she didn't utter a sound. Around maybe two weeks later, she beckoned me. And she said something to me, which I don't really remember-- something that she is preparing me for something. There was, around the corner, when they ran up-- around the corner, entering the little kitchenette, there was like a narrow cupboard. It was a very high, narrow, dark brown, solid cupboard. And in this cupboard, one of drawers-- I could reach up. And I was very small, so it was not on the lower. But it was somewhere on the higher-- there was a false, little drawer, which I assume my father-- I would-- or it was the-- or my father made-- a very, just, you know, literally sort of-- sort of touching-- you know, just very small. And there was a hook-- just you had to put your hand inside around there. On the left-hand side, there was like a little hook there that if you-- it was not visible. You couldn't see anything. But if you unhooked that, the whole-- this-- it all dropped onto the next one. So whatever bits and pieces there was, it was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Lilka said to me, you think that I didn't know? I knew that it was there. I knew all along that it was there. I just could not believe it-- that she could, at her age-- what was she-- 8, 8 and a 1/2 --that she could-- that she took-- that she understood. That she took. And later on they started to bribe her. You see, they said, we give you-- they changed, you know? We could-- we give you this, we give. Nothing will happen, you know? She was a child, you know, a child, after all. She didn't say anything that she took it. So, speaking about the guts and the courage of Jewish children-- if she would have said-- if she would have pointed-- as a matter of fact, I remember there was something at that time. There was something like with a sapphire, a little sapphire with something. Who-- you know-- you know if that-- I mean, I can't even tell you what would have happened. You know? So, somehow, they didn't see it. They didn't enter the mind to look for something which was-- for whatever, they didn't find it. She didn't say it. And, to me, you know, it is a part of my being. This-- this, what she did then, it's a part of my being, you know? It's sort of like my-- my-- in my heart, beating, you know? That I couldn't-- I couldn't believe it. I couldn't understand this, you know, that she didn't-- we didn't talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never. After this, I never went to my father-- never went. My father never brought anything. Or, if he did, my mother didn't know. My mother-- nobody knew about it. I never told my mother or father what she did, what she knew. And my father never did. My mother never knew about this at all. It was between my father and myself. But somehow, the perception that-- that the-- how she knew-- but somehow she knew where. And somehow she don't know how, but she knew. I never sort of asked her how and what. She never talked about the letter. She just wanted to tell me. She needed to tell me that she knew all along.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that this jewelry and coins that your father brought, you would take out and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --trade. How did you conceal this?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my body.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wore always something that had a little pocket. And I had something like a-- they used to have then-- you know when you wear pajamas, sort of like bands, you know? And it sort of-- I sort of-- by that time, I was very adept, you know? I knew exactly how, and what, and when. It was not out of the ghetto, but it was something like-- it was a way there, where this man who was a Pole-- where this man and my father-- my father didn't-- he perhaps, he felt that the less I know the better. And I didn't ask him. It was not important. Whatever my father said, I knew I trusted him 100%. And I knew that it had to be done this way. And I knew it was between-- he said it was between you and me. And, of course, this meant-- and nobody else knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe how the ghetto was surrounded-- by what sort of structure? Was it a wall? Was it barbed wire? How was this done?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember any barbed wires. I just remember that it was confined to a few streets and that my whole life revolved going in the morning to [NON-ENGLISH], coming back. I had just this way-- getting up in the morning, going for the bread, going to lessons, coming back, reading something. If there were something to eat, eating something, going again to lessons, coming back. This is really-- you know-- the rest, it's sort of-- at that time, this time, in the ghetto, somehow it was-- where the details somehow are not engraved, you know, in my mind. It is just-- things had to be done. You went, and you did it. And you sort of-- it was-- it was a struggle from second to second. You didn't really know in that next second what is going to happen, how it's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, as a matter of fact, Olkusz was one of the first ghettos that was liquidated. You see? So it not that it was till 1943, like Bedzin was, or like-- like till 1944, like Lodz was. It was just one of the first ghettos that was liquidated. How long-- how long did you stay in the ghetto in Olkusz-- you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, altogether-- since we came there-- altogether it would have been some-- close to something like two years and seven months-- something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the events leading up to you leaving the Olkusz ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. If I may, I would. My father went back to Bedzin for the two weeks. We went with him at this time. And he went. What he told us later on-- much, much later on-- a couple of months later on-- it is that he had a very strange dream. And I was putting emphasis on this balcony, where we had-- in the second flat, we had this balcony and those glass doors. He dreamt that my mother went out on the balcony with her arms outstretched, and that she pleaded with him to help her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he having this dream?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bedzin. And he-- the-- his father was there. He was there with his father. You see, they let him be there with his father and with his mother-- only cut him off from his then immediate-- from his family. Because apparently, this they felt he could work. And otherwise, you know, he would be too-- too involved with-- with-- with his children or something there. And-- and my grandfather, who happened to be a very logical-- regardless of-- first, he applied logic. And he said, look. You love your wife. Your love your kids. You miss them. You think of her. You dreamt! So he accepted it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the same grandfather who read the stories to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. This is the only grandfather I have. This is-- that was my paternal grandfather-- [NON-ENGLISH] Mayer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, carry on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had this-- and then twice more, he dreamt exactly the same dream. And he said to his father he is a bit embarrassed. I mean, after all, he doesn't believe in dreams, you know, either. But it's just something about this dream that he's very disturbed about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father said, look. If you are so worried about it, go to the treuhander, and perhaps-- perhaps he will let you go. See what you can do. I'm sure that it is all right. But, you know, if it worries you to that extent-- and we went to him. And whatever he told him, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, suddenly, there he is, in the afternoon. And we got a fright. By seeing him, we got a fright, because he just went back three or four days before. And he didn't say anything. And, at that stage, everything was as it was. I mean, you know, it seemed to-- sort of-- you know, every day with a-- with a missing, with this. But we were-- the three of us were together. And at that time, the other people, all of them, they didn't take away. Perhaps for the last few weeks they didn't take away the other people, either. They just let them be. Because, after all, they prepared for that. And so he said, look. I don't know. But I think I just made a mistake. I think if I go back now, I can come again for two days. Otherwise, you know, otherwise it will be maybe four weeks before I can-- I can come.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, he felt perhaps silly. He didn't tell us, but he must have felt silly then, at that time-- that moment. And we-- so we said, OK. Can we go with you to the train? He said, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know how it happened that the train went early. Instead of 5 to 4:00, we were there earlier-- something-- perhaps something-- now, when I talk about it, it could have had something to do with this, what was going to happen next. Because the train went much earlier, and he missed the train. So he said, OK, 5 o'clock in the morning. So he went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was happening at 5 o'clock in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between 2:00 and 3:00, we heard running of the boots. We heard a lot of noise-- shouting, shoving, all kinds of things. The beginning of liquidation of the ghetto happened. Now, in the next three days-- [SIGH] --they were all-- I wasn't there with my parents. Because my father-- straight away, when they took us, they make like a makeshift-- it was in a building. It was-- again, it was not in the ghetto. It was somewhere on the outskirts. I can see the trees. I can see it all, but not, of course, for a long time. And I'll show you the picture I've got of the liquidation of the ghetto. And my father said to me, you go with me now. There's like a little room, like a makeshift hospital. There are people who are sick on top of it. But there is-- there was a curtain or under the bed-- this is where you hide. And he said to me then, remember, I promise you, whatever-- we won't be separated. Whatever will happen to one will happen to the rest of us. Whatever will be, we will together for always. So we don't worry. Just stay where you are. And this is where I was for the next three days-- or under the bed, or behind the-- where it was something like a curtain or whatever. There was nothing there. I don't remember getting any food. Of course, I wouldn't dare to-- whatever. I don't remember-- on top, on the beds, there, I remember just-- I don't remember the sounds. Although there was-- people were groaning and moaning. They were not shot. Perhaps by that time, they felt that-- that the-- that the bullets are too good for the Jews-- by that time. Because they knew what they're going to do anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then, in the third day, my father came in, and he said, quickly-- he had something like a-- a very thick-- it wasn't a sheet. It was like linen-- a piece of linen. And I was, of course, like a bag of bones-- very little. And he said, just hop in here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took me over-- like a bundle, over his shoulder. And what it must have happened-- it is that somehow-- because my-- if my father would not have been there, at that time, there is no way that I would have sit here today in front of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they would have-- there were-- all apart from 70 people, who were somewhat useful in Bedzin. So somehow, when he came-- and with one child-- and somehow he felt-- he didn't explain to me that he felt maybe that if they will see two children, maybe I was old enough to be taken away to-- to the-- to a camp, you know? Are you saying that's why he hid you and not--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Lilka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. That's what I think. I can't find a different reason. Because Lilka was certainly lighter than I was. She was 1/2 of my-- you know, as little as I was, she was much smaller. But the thing is that he stood there with me, over his-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --left shoulder, most of the time. I don't know why. And when his turn came--

speaker: interviewer
text: A turn came for what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Turn-- they were just taking the people, you know? So the 70 people that they took to Bedzin-- the people went past like a selection-- segregation. Yes? This was-- this was the Judenrein. This was the Judenrein-- the liquidation of the ghetto Bedzin. When it was my father's turn, what he said-- it is where he were, what it is, you know, one-- that's what he said. Somehow, how it escaped them, the outline-- there was nothing there but-- but I. I, till today, I don't-- I talk-- talk about this again and again. I do not understand this at all how they didn't look, how they didn't prod. He took the chance, and they let us through.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you please explain-- who was with your father? You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my mother, and Lilka. And I was-- he was holding me in a-- in a bundle over his shoulder. And, as unbelievable, even when I talk about it, it's hard for me to believe that actually this-- but it's just his quick thinking and the chance that he took. And because he made a promise to me, that, no matter what, we'll be never separated, when it happened, I-- I think-- I think I was angry. I was very angry. Because he said, we'll never be separated. And, in my child that I was, I believed-- I believed that was my father-- he was on this pedestal. To me, he was-- he could manage anything. He could fix anything. And, you know, so-- and this is how we went into Bedzin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- why were you angry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you later. This is-- this is for later, really-- when it happened later. When we were all together, of course it was unbelievable. We were-- because, you know, there is no way. There is no way that they would have-- [SIGH] --that my mother could have-- that they would have let my mother with the two-- there's no way. But because he was there, and because he had a certain presence. I don't know. And he will-- he spoke with conviction-- that it is-- and I think that that must have been something there from Bedzin that, you know, maybe they did something. Maybe they felt, you know, maybe they felt that they're going to be-- because there were-- the-- the-- the guards, you know, they had to be so correct if something happens. I mean, they had to know. They had to be 100%. They just couldn't take any chances this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even, you know, even they were so careful, one in front of the other, you know, not to say or do anything that would put certain that could endanger them in a way, that would have contribute to the-- the whatever. You know? So they-- they were very careful in what they discussed. So in the liquidation of the ghetto, you said 70 people-- about.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 70 people-- the rest stands there and waits for the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many was the rest? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Close to something like over 4,000. So I assume altogether it would be 4,500 Jews most of the time. And they were standing there-- calm, waiting. They were told.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they told? That you're going to be resettled to a place where there will be more work. There will be better living facilities. And, you know, you will be-- and they all-- if they believed it or not, they wanted to believe it. By the way they're standing, they can see that they wanted to believe it. But this is what they were told, apparently, you know, not to panic, but to go peacefully inside the wagons-- very simply. You know? So they took the people that went to Bedzin. And the others just went. Where did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, probably to where-- where the rest went-- to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and your family, as a result of your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --hiding you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when did your father let you out of the sack across his shoulder?

speaker: interviewee
text: He really didn't let me out till we were in Bedzin-- all throughout. Now I-- this-- I don't-- I don't really-- in Bedzin, when we came, I mean, I couldn't see it. I just know that there was some exchange when-- when we arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: But perhaps he sort of-- the others were carrying bundles as well. Everybody was allowed, you know, for the sake of appearances, to take a bundle. So you will need it, you know? So when you come to your destination, you will have this bundle with you. You see?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but after we went, one of the streets there, then, he let me out. And, of course, we were-- it was an indescribable feeling. You can't describe it. You know, we were together. We were together. And this, that's all that matters-- that all that mattered that for that time, we were together. And it seemed, by that time-- [SIGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: --it seemed-- it went on endlessly. You know? That was already close to three years after the breakout of the war, you know? So it seemed sort of endlessly.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow, again, when you are a child, there is the structure, and there is somehow continuity. But when I came to Bedzin, all of this finished. I mean, the lessons, the-- all of this finished. There was nothing thereafter. And my mother was supposed to go to work. Everybody had to work then. That was a finishing factory where you finish the uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, again, I wouldn't let my mother go. And so I went instead of my mother. They couldn't-- they wouldn't know, you know, as long as there was some-- they wouldn't know if I was 12, or if I was 14, or if I was 20, you know? At that stage. I mean, it changed-- there were-- by that time, in Bedzin, there were selections, and there were separations.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but somehow, by-- somehow, before they took me to camp, nobody-- nobody perished-- yet. At that stage, we knew that our uncles and uncle were in Krakow. We knew, you know, that one-- I knew, after all, of course, that one cousin was-- went-- Halinka, who lives in Israel-- that she-- that she went to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because somebody came. In Olkusz then, there was, as well my-- my grandfather's brother lived in Olkusz. And he was sort of-- it was my great-uncle. But I didn't know him very well from before the war, really, because he was in Vienna, really. He just came after he became a grandfather. His name was David. And after he became a grandfather, he decided that he contributed to the-- that he perpetuated, you know, the Jewish-- the continuity, and that he became a grandfather. He was a very Orthodox Jew. And he wanted to study. He wanted to go to university. And so he did. And by the time the war broke out, he was a doctor of philosophy. He got his doctorate. But he couldn't do it in Poland, so the whole family went to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by-- he was back. And, you know, I saw him nearly every day. So he was-- I couldn't see my grandparents, you know, but I saw him. So he was like-- I can't say a substitute, but in a way, I was not his-- really, his like his great-niece. I was more like a granddaughter, you know? Did he have the same surname as your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. They were brothers, yes. He was the older brother, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about arriving in Bedzin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- how was daily life there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- again, in the morning for bread, then going for the whole day to work. I was there nearly three months-- close to three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was the factory from where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't very far. I mean, I really can't-- 10 minutes, 15. But it was within-- there was not really-- the ghetto in Bedzin was not yet the real ghetto. And Bedzin-- I was not really terribly familiar with Bedzin. Although, I was there every week. Because we went straight away to my grandparents. And as soon as I came in, there was all-- there were all this family, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and all I wanted is hide somewhere where they would give me a little bit of peace, where I could read or whatever. Because before I even opened my mouth, I was already a genius, you know? This is-- and this is before the war, you're talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was before the war. And even when I was-- during the war, I mean, of course, we were such close proximity and all the time and-- with each others. But it still-- the nurturing-- you know, my grandfather was my grandfather, and my grandmother was still acting in the way. But when I was at work, I was an adult. And I had to do the work of an adult. And then-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, we're just going to stop there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and change tapes.
